title: The Return from Egypt
subtitle: The weary road home through the wilderness, where the boy John already does penance

stillness: Be still, and rest from earthly cares.
For those who rest from worldly business
are made worthy to delight in the sight of Angels.
gospel: behold, an Angel of the Lord appeared to Joseph in a dream in Egypt,
saying: Arise, and take the Child and his Mother,
and go into the land of Israel.
For they are dead that sought the life of the Child.
And he arose, and took the Child and his Mother,
and came into the land of Israel.
behold: They go, then, and cross through the desert, the same by which they had come.
Go, you also, with them, lending them help and service,
and spending yourself for them in whatever you can.
And on the very road you can have compassion on them,
and look upon them, wearied and overcome by toil,
having little rest, by day as well as by night.
O how greatly this noble and tender Child, the King of heaven and earth,
labored for us, and how early he began!
Near the edge of that desert, the boy John the Baptist had already begun to do penance,
though he bore no sin at all.
He abode in the desert, where the air is purer, the sky more open, and God more near.
consider: Great want, hard toils, and harsh afflictions of the body he most steadfastly took up,
and gave himself, as though in hatred of himself, for love of us.
Surely this labor alone, of which we now treat,
ought to have sufficed for our full redemption.
In his person the Prophet foretold:
I am poor, and in labors from my youth.
The future preacher of penance, as soon as ever he could,
chose and sought the harshness of solitude,
that, living in the wilderness, he might lead an austere life,
and the more easily lift himself, and his hearers, away from the love and enticements of the world.
He fled the crowds and the tumult of the people,
lest from dwelling among them he should contract some stain of scandal or sin.
For, as Chrysostom says: as it is impossible for a tree set beside the road
to keep its fruit unspoiled until it ripens,
so it is for a man set beside the world to keep his innocence to the end.
He fled the world, that he might guard his holiness,
and not, even by a light word, defile his life.
Good is it, then, by his example, to bear the yoke of the Lord from youth,
and, while nature is tender, to accustom oneself to good.
prayer: who, born of the Virgin, withdrew into the regions of Egypt,
and, called out of Egypt, returned into the land of Israel:
call me back, O Lord, your servant,
cast out from before the sight of your eyes into the Egypt of this exile,
banished in the darkness of this pilgrimage.
From the regions of Egypt, from the darkness of worldly living,
lead me back in body, lead me back in mind, lead me back in will.
Make me forsake my vices, and pass over to the virtues,
and deign to bring me into the land of promise:
now by faith, hope, and charity;
but in time to come, in reality, in vision, in glory, and in truth. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: O quantum Puer egregius ac delicatus, Rex cæli et terræ, pro nobis laboravit, et quam cito cæperit!
latinJewel.english: O how greatly this noble and tender Child, the King of heaven and earth, labored for us, and how early he began!
